ctx: scan for open TCP ports on 10.0.0.5
why: -sU runs a UDP scan, not a TCP scan, so it will not find open TCP ports.
fixWhy: -sT does a TCP connect scan to find open TCP ports (-sS SYN scan also works but needs root).

ctx: capture packets on ens33 with timestamps
why: -t SUPPRESSES the timestamp on every line, which defeats the goal of capturing with timestamps.
fixWhy: -tt prints an unformatted (epoch) timestamp per packet; -t removes timestamps entirely.

ctx: list the open TCP ports on target.local
why: -sU runs a UDP scan, not a TCP scan, so it will not find open TCP ports.
fixWhy: -sT does a TCP connect scan to find open TCP ports (-sS SYN scan also works but needs root).

ctx: scan for open TCP ports on target.local
why: -sU runs a UDP scan, not a TCP scan, so it will not find open TCP ports.
fixWhy: -sT does a TCP connect scan to find open TCP ports (-sS SYN scan also works but needs root).

ctx: capture packets on eth0 with timestamps
why: -t SUPPRESSES the timestamp on every line, which defeats the goal of capturing with timestamps.
fixWhy: -tt prints an unformatted (epoch) timestamp per packet; -t removes timestamps entirely.

ctx: scan for open TCP ports on 127.0.0.1
why: -sU runs a UDP scan, not a TCP scan, so it will not find open TCP ports.
fixWhy: -sT does a TCP connect scan to find open TCP ports (-sS SYN scan also works but needs root).

ctx: capture packets on wlan0 with timestamps
why: -t SUPPRESSES the timestamp on every line, which defeats the goal of capturing with timestamps.
fixWhy: -tt prints an unformatted (epoch) timestamp per packet; -t removes timestamps entirely.

ctx: read traffic.pcap and keep only tcp packets
why: -f is a capture (BPF) filter that only applies to a live capture; reading a file needs a display filter.
fixWhy: -Y applies a Wireshark display filter when reading a pcap; -f only works during live capture.

ctx: extract HTTP request host+URI from traffic.pcap without name resolution
code: tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y http.request -T fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri
why: Without -n, tshark resolves addresses via DNS — slow, and it leaks lookups for the very hosts you are analysing.
fixLines: tshark ___ -r traffic.pcap -Y http.request -T fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri
fixWhy: -n disables name resolution: faster analysis and no DNS leakage.

ctx: reconstruct TCP flows on port 443 into /var/tmp/cap/
why: tcpflow takes its filter as a bare BPF expression (port 443); -p is the no-promiscuous flag, so '-p 443' is not a port filter.
fixWhy: Filters are bare BPF: 'port 443'. (-o correctly sets the output directory.)

ctx: reassemble streams on port 80 with tcpflow
why: tcpflow takes its filter as a bare BPF expression (port 80); -p is the no-promiscuous flag, so '-p 80' is not a port filter.
fixWhy: Filters are bare BPF: 'port 80'. (-o correctly sets the output directory.)

ctx: list listening TCP ports with their process, without slow reverse-DNS
why: Without -n, netstat resolves addresses and ports via reverse DNS, which is slow and noisy.
fixWhy: -n shows numeric addresses/ports, avoiding slow reverse-DNS lookups.

ctx: list the open TCP ports on target.local
why: -sU runs a UDP scan, not a TCP scan, so it will not find open TCP ports.
fixWhy: -sT does a TCP connect scan to find open TCP ports (-sS SYN scan also works but needs root).

ctx: read capture.pcap and show HTTP requests without slow DNS lookups
code: tshark -r capture.pcap -Y http.request -T fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri
why: Without -n, tshark resolves addresses via DNS — slow, and it leaks lookups for the very hosts you are analysing.
fixLines: tshark ___ -r capture.pcap -Y http.request -T fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri
fixWhy: -n disables name resolution: faster analysis and no DNS leakage.

ctx: find the hostname behind 192.168.1.1
why: -r only tells dig to skip ~/.digrc; it does NOT perform a reverse lookup.
fixWhy: -x builds the in-addr.arpa PTR query automatically for a reverse lookup.

ctx: filter a saved capture capture.pcap down to tls
why: -f is a capture (BPF) filter that only applies to a live capture; reading a file needs a display filter.
fixWhy: -Y applies a Wireshark display filter when reading a pcap; -f only works during live capture.

fixWhy: Filters are bare BPF: 'port 8080'. (-o correctly sets the output directory.)

ctx: filter a saved capture traffic.pcap down to dns
why: -f is a capture (BPF) filter that only applies to a live capture; reading a file needs a display filter.
